---
title: "Mini Inquiry Presentation"
subtitle: "[Research question goes here]"
author: "[Student name]"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, inquiry-theme.scss]
    slide-number: true
    transition: none
    width: 1050
    height: 700
    fig-align: center
    code-fold: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

## Research Question

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
State your research question in 1–2 sentences. It should connect a positive psychology concept to a characteristic that varies across people.
:::

<!-- Example: Does the relationship between gratitude practice and
reported life satisfaction differ by cultural background? -->

## Why This Question

- Which course readings motivated this question?
- Which theoretical framework does it operationalise?
- Why does the characteristic you chose matter?

::: {.notes}
Cite at least two course readings here. This is where CLO 1
(theoretical frameworks) and CLO 3 (academic texts) are assessed.
:::

## Participant Design

- **Sample size:** [N] participants
- **Key characteristics:** [list with distributions and rationale]
- **Independent variable:** [how participants were split across conditions]

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
Explain one design choice and why you made it. For example: "I split cultural backgrounds into equal thirds rather than proportional to the student body because..."
:::

## Survey Instrument

- **Constructs measured:** [e.g., gratitude (8 items), life satisfaction (6 items)]
- **Scale:** [e.g., 1–7 Likert]
- **Reverse-scored items:** [how many and why]

Show 1–2 example items to illustrate what you measured.

## Effect Model

What effects did you build into the simulation, and why?

| Effect | Size | Justification |
|--------|------|---------------|
| [Main effect] | [e.g., +0.8] | [Reading and reasoning] |
| [Moderation] | [e.g., −0.3] | [Reading and reasoning] |

::: {.notes}
Every number here is a claim about the world. Be ready to
defend each one from the course readings.
:::

## Prior Predictive Check

Did your priors produce plausible predictions?

```{python}
# Replace with your prior predictive plot code
# The LLM that ran your analysis can provide this
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
Describe what the plot shows. Do the predictions stay within 1–7? Is there pileup at floor or ceiling?
:::

## Fake Data Recovery

Did the analysis pipeline recover the known parameters?

```{python}
# Replace with your parameter recovery plot code
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
Do the points fall near the diagonal? Which parameters were recovered well? Which were not, and why?
:::

## Results: Posteriors

```{python}
# Replace with your posterior density plot code
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
For each effect, report: posterior median, 89% credible interval, probability that the effect is in the expected direction.
:::

## Results: Prior to Posterior

```{python}
# Replace with your prior-to-posterior comparison plot code
```

What did the data "teach" the model? Where did beliefs shift most?

## Results: Group Means

```{python}
# Replace with your predicted group means plot code
```

This is the headline result. Describe the pattern and the remaining uncertainty.

## Type S and Type M

For each effect:

| Effect | P(wrong sign) | Exaggeration ratio |
|--------|---------------|--------------------|
| [Main effect] | [e.g., 6%] | [e.g., 1.2×] |
| [Moderation] | [e.g., 32%] | [e.g., 2.1×] |

What do these tell you about how confident you should be?

## Posterior Predictive Check

```{python}
# Replace with your posterior predictive overlay plot code
```

Does the model capture the shape of the data?

## Sensitivity Analysis

```{python}
# Replace with your sensitivity comparison plot code
```

What did you change, and what happened? How sensitive are your conclusions to your assumptions?

## Discussion

- What do the patterns suggest about your research question?
- What assumptions drive the result?
- What would you do differently with real participants?

## Limitations

::: {.incremental}
- The data reflects the effect model I built, not human psychology.
- My priors influence the posteriors: [explain how].
- [If applicable:] The LLM helped set effect sizes, which may encode stereotypes.
- The simulation cannot surprise the way real data can.
:::

::: {.notes}
This slide is where the deepest thinking lives. Do not rush it.
:::

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
